data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Software Stocks Slammed
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Software stocks slide sharply as Microsoft, ServiceNow stoke fears. (0:15) Caterpillar beats earnings as AI data-center demand lifts sales. (1:57) Barry Diller wants CNN. (2:59)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, software struggles are getting hardcore.

The sector is selling off sharply, taking tech and the broader market down with it. The iShares Expanded Tech-Software ETF (IGV) is down more than 5% — a drop we haven’t seen since tariff Liberation Day.

That’s dragged down the Nasdaq (COMP.IND), while bitcoin (BTC-USD), gold (GLD) and silver (SLV) also saw simultaneous selling. All those asset classes are now off their lows.

At the coalface of the software slump is Microsoft (MSFT), which you heard on Wall Street Breakfast was sinking despite solid, if unspectacular, quarterly results. Selling has ramped up through the session, with the stock off about 12% — on track for the seventh-worst decline in its history.

Evercore analyst Kirk Materne says the debate is “no longer about demand; it is about capacity timing (and perhaps allocation).”

“While Azure growth at these levels remains impressive and continues to suggest market share gains, capex rose 66% year over year, and investors are increasingly looking for clearer evidence that this elevated investment is translating into incremental Azure acceleration,” he added.

The real trigger, though, may have been ServiceNow (NOW), down more than 10% despite strong results.

Analysts noted that 21.5% subscription revenue growth “did not quite represent acceleration,” but were still broadly positive. Citizens reiterated its Market Outperform rating, pointing to revenue growth, free cash flow margin and a strong leadership team.

Even so, the stock has now shed about 50% of its value over the past year.

Also heavily in the red are Atlassian (TEAM), Salesforce (CRM), Workday (WDAY) and DataDog (DOG).

Among other active stocks, Caterpillar (CAT) continues to benefit from AI — specifically data center demand — with Q4 results beating expectations.

CAT said fourth-quarter sales rose to $19.1B, above the $17.76B consensus. Adjusted EPS came in at $5.16, easily topping the $4.71 expected.

Power and energy sales climbed 23% to $9.4B. Within that segment, power generation revenue jumped 44% to $3.24B, driven by higher sales of large reciprocating engines that the company said were primarily tied to data-center applications, as the AI buildout keeps echoing through demand.

Altria (MO) is under pressure after slightly missing profit estimates in Q4. The tobacco giant said it’s targeting mid-single-digit annual dividend-per-share growth through 2028. Altria has boosted its dividend 60 times in the last 56 years.

And Joby Aviation (JOBY) is sinking after pricing an offering. The electric vertical take-off-and-landing company will issue $600M of 0.75% convertible senior notes due 2032 and sell about 52.9M shares of common stock at $11.35 per share.

And in other news of note, Barry Diller has expressed interest in buying CNN and approached Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) about a deal last year.

The Wall Street Journal reports that Diller inquired about CNN before Warner announced its plan to split into two distinct businesses — and before WBD formally put itself up for sale.

Sources close to Warner told the Journal that no serious action was taken on Diller’s approach, and it wasn’t brought to the board. But Diller is still said to be interested in CNN and is eyeing a deal in a personal capacity.

data.title: AgomAb Therapeutics NV Readies US IPO On Early Promising Results
data.description:
A Quick Take On AgomAb

AgomAb Therapeutics NV (AGMB) has filed to raise $200 million in development capital in an IPO of its American Depositary Shares representing underlying common shares, according to SEC F-1/A registration information.

The company is a clinical-stage biopharma advancing treatment candidates for chronic fibrotic conditions.

It has achieved positive safety results and early promising signals on exploratory clinical endpoints.

Given these early results, its strong institutional life science VC, and strategic big pharma investor backing, the IPO will likely see significant institutional investor demand.

For patient life science investors, my outlook on the AGMB IPO is a Buy at up to $16.00 per share.

AgomAb Overview and Development Efforts

Antwerpen, Belgium-based AgomAb Therapeutics NV is developing chronic fibrosis treatments for severe conditions "through inhibition of one of the key signaling pathways involved in fibrosis, the transforming growth factor β, or TGFβ, pathway."

Management is headed by Chief Executive Officer and Executive Director, Mr. Tim Knotnerus, who has been with the firm since February 2019 and has held senior positions at AM-Pharma B.V. and was a Senior Associate at Aescap Venture, a European venture capital fund investing in European medical companies.

The company's lead treatment candidate, AGMB-129 (Ontunisertib), is in Phase 2a trials for the treatment of fibrostenosing Crohn's Disease.

Management expects to have full open-label extension data in the second half of 2026.

AGMB is in Phase 1 trials for AGMB-447 for the treatment of idiopathic (unknown origin) pulmonary fibrosis.

The status of the company’s current development pipeline is shown below:

AgomAb has recorded a fair market value investment of $365 million through September 30, 2025, from investors, including LSP, Fidelity Management & Research, Pontifax, Sanofi Foreign Participations (SNY), Redmile Biopharma Investments, Cormorant Asset Management, Pfizer (PFE), and Canaan.

AgomAb’s Markets & Competitors

Per a 2025 market research report by Data Bridge Market Research, the global market for fibrotic disease treatments was an estimated $5.8 billion in 2024 and is expected to exceed $10.3 billion by 2032.

This represents a forecast CAGR of 7.6% from 2025 to 2032.

The main reasons for this expected growth are a growing prevalence of fibrotic diseases due to aging populations, lifestyle alterations, and chronic contributing conditions such as hypertension and diabetes.

Also, the chart below shows the historical and projected future growth path of the fibrotic disease treatment market through 2030 by region:

Major competitive companies that have developed or are developing similar treatments include the following firms:

  • Merck (MRK)

  • Sanofi

  • Roche Holding (RHHBY)

  • Boehringer Ingelheim International GmbH

  • Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY)

  • Amgen (AMGN)

  • Numerous others

The fibrotic condition treatment market has typically focused on managing symptoms or inflammation.

AgomAb’s treatment approach is to focus on restoring tissue homeostasis by reprogramming or inhibiting activated fibroblasts back to an inactive state.

AGMB’s pipeline is designed to be organ-agnostic, with potential applicability in GI, liver, and other fibrotic indications.

AgomAb Financial Results

AgomAb’s recent financial results are typical of a development-stage biopharma firm in that they feature high R&D and G&A costs associated with their pipeline advancement efforts and no revenue.

According to its current IPO filing, the table below shows the company's financial results for the periods indicated:

As of September 30, 2025, the company had $151.6 million in cash and $11.1 million in total liabilities.

AgomAb IPO Details

AgomAb will sell 12.5 million American Depositary Shares representing underlying common shares at a proposed midpoint price of $16.00 per share for gross proceeds of $200 million.

The company didn’t disclose any proposed institutional purchasers of stock in the IPO, whether from existing or new investors.

Shareholder, company, and executive shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the normal & customary 180 days after the effective date of the IPO prospectus.

Management is asking IPO investors to value the company at an enterprise value of approximately $497 million, within the typical range of a high-quality biotech firm at IPO, so the valuation appears reasonable.

AgomAb said it intends to use the IPO net proceeds as described below:

to advance clinical development of ontunisertib, including for the planned global Phase 2b trial in FSCD patients;

to advance clinical development of AGMB-447, including for the planned Phase 2 trial in IPF patients; and

the remainder to fund advancement of preclinical candidates, general and administrative expenses, working capital, one milestone payment pursuant to our acquisition agreement relating to Origo Biopharma, S.L. and other general corporate purposes.

(Source: SEC)

Leadership’s online company roadshow presentation is not available to the public.

Regarding legal proceedings against the firm or its directors or employees, management said the company is not presently a party to any legal proceedings of a material amount.

The listed bookrunners of the IPO are J.P. Morgan, Morgan Stanley, Leerink Partners, and Van Lanschot Kempen.

My Thoughts About AgomAb’s IPO

AGMB is seeking US public market capital to fund advancing its programs for serious, chronic fibrotic treatments.

The firm’s lead candidate, AGMB-129 (Ontunisertib), is in Phase 2a trials for the treatment of fibrostenosing Crohn's Disease.

AgomAb said it plans to have full open-label extension data in the second half of 2026.

For its lead program, its Phase 2a trial results reached its primary endpoint for safety and tolerability and “also observed positive signals on several exploratory clinical endpoints.”

The market opportunity for fibrotic condition treatments is large and expected to grow at a moderate rate of growth, but the company faces significant competition from major market participants.

AGMB hasn’t disclosed any major pharma firm collaboration agreements or relationships.

However, the company’s investor syndicate includes strategic investors Sanofi and Pfizer, along with several well-known institutional life science venture capital firms, and is well-capitalized.

It is uncommon for a biopharma to have two strategic investors and is noteworthy.

Other risks for investors include its ‘foreign private issuer’ and ‘emerging growth company’ status, which lets management disclose substantially less information to investors, at its option.

Many such company stocks have performed poorly post-IPO, although they have not typically been in the life science industry.

Given the company's progress to date, including promising early efficacy results and its strong institutional life science venture capital and strategic investor backing, the IPO will likely be in strong demand from institutional investors.

For life science investors with a patient hold time frame, my outlook on the AGMB IPO is a Buy at up to $16.00 per share.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: February 5, 2026

data.title: FAX: Fixed-Income Fund With Primary Exposure To Asia-Pacific Region
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The last time we gave the abrdn Asia-Pacific Income Fund (FAX) a look, it was touching on the potential merger of abrdn Global Income Fund Inc (FCO) into FAX. This would make FAX slightly larger in terms of total managed assets than it is today. However, FCO is quite a small fund at around $60 million AUM compared to FAX, standing at over $1.1 billion. With that, it would be relatively immaterial to FAX. It would easily liquidate the current portfolio and absorb it into FAX's own underlying strategy.

In that piece, I highlighted mostly FCO and how we viewed FCO as a 'Sell' because of its enormous premium. When that merger was announced, it was a very rapid decline for FCO. This was because FAX did not trade at even close to the same valuation that FCO had at the time.

So FCO sank rapidly to meet the same level that FAX was trading at. Since that announcement, FAX continues to trade at a wider discount, but not at a level that makes it attractive enough to consider adding aggressively, in my opinion. On the other hand, the fund's rather high distribution rate is likely enticing to some investors—the problem on that front is that the coverage of the distribution is rather weak in terms of income. Therefore, the fund will rely on requiring capital gains to fund its full distribution, or it would risk seeing NAV erode.

FAX Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.07
  • Discount/Premium: -5.66%
  • Distribution Yield: 12.77%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.41%
  • Leverage: 38.58%
  • Managed Assets: $1.104 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

FAX's investment objective is to "seek current income". In an effort to meet that investment objective, the fund will:

normally invest at least 80% of its total assets, plus the amount of any borrowings for investment purposes, in "Asia-Pacific debt securities,” which include: (1) debt securities of Asia-Pacific Country issuers, including securities issued by Asia-Pacific Country governmental entities, as well as by banks, companies and other entities which are located in Asia-Pacific Countries, whether or not denominated in an Asia-Pacific Country currency; (2) debt securities of other issuers, denominated in, or linked to, the currency of an Asia-Pacific Country, including securities issued by supranational issuers, such as The World Bank and derivative debt securities that replicate, or substitute for, the currency of an Asia-Pacific Country; (3) debt securities issued by entities which, although not located in an Asia-Pacific Country, derive at least 50% of their revenues from Asia-Pacific Countries or have at least 50% of their assets located in Asia-Pacific Countries; and (4) debt securities issued by a wholly-owned subsidiary of an entity located in an Asia-Pacific Country, provided that the debt securities are guaranteed by the parent entity located in the Asia-Pacific Country (the “80% Policy”). With reference to items (3) and (4) above, Asia-Pacific debt securities may be denominated in an Asia-Pacific Country currency or U.S. dollars. “Asia-Pacific Countries” (each, an “Asia-Pacific Country”) means countries included in “Asia” and “Oceania” in the United Nations (“UN”) geographic regions used by the UN Statistics Division.

When including the leverage expenses, which this fund is fairly heavily leveraged, the total expense ratio comes in at 4.22%. That was actually up substantially from last year's 3.31%.

They operate with a credit facility, and that comes with a floating rate-based debt. The latest annual report showed it coming in at 5.87% on average for fiscal 2025. As the Fed eases its target rate, that will help to also ease the borrowing costs for FAX. That's why it is still interesting that despite the 75 basis points of cuts at the end of 2024, expenses still rose.

They also have some senior secured notes and preferred shares that they employ for leverage, and these come with attractive fixed rates. Below are the senior notes and the interest rates associated with those. The Series B Mandatory Redeemable Preferred Shares come in with an annual dividend of 5.391%.

That would have helped to keep some of their borrowing costs from seeing an increase. Of course, it also means now that a large portion of their leverage also won't benefit from reduced rates.

FAX Acquiring FCO Isn't Guaranteed

Interesting to note on the potential FCO merger into FAX is that they've also included in the latest filing that there is also a plan of liquidation potentially that FCO investors can also vote on. In that case, the merger with FAX would not end up going through, and FCO would simply liquidate at NAV.

1. Agreement and Plan of Reorganization. To approve an Agreement and Plan of Reorganization providing for the transfer of all of the assets of the Acquired Fund to abrdn Asia-Pacific Income Fund, Inc. (the “Acquiring Fund”) in exchange solely for newly issued common stock of the Acquiring Fund (although cash may be distributed in lieu of fractional shares) and the assumption by the Acquiring Fund of all or substantially all liabilities of the Acquired Fund and the distribution of common stock of the Acquiring Fund to the stockholders of the Acquired Fund and complete liquidation of the Acquired Fund (the “Reorganization”). The Acquiring Fund as it would exist after the Reorganization is referred to as the “Combined Fund.”

2. Plan of Liquidation. To approve a plan of liquidation and dissolution (the “Liquidation Plan”) of the Acquired Fund (the “Liquidation”). The Liquidation will only occur if the Liquidation Plan is approved by stockholders of the Acquired Fund and the Reorganization is not approved by stockholders of the Acquired Fund or is not consummated for any other reason. If only the Reorganization Agreement is approved, or if both the Reorganization Agreement and the Liquidation Plan are approved, the Reorganization will occur.

The special meeting isn't being held until March 12, 2026, so investors have quite a bit of time to decide. However, at the present time, actually voting for liquidation for FCO investors makes the most sense. This is because while they'd see immediate downside for accepting the current NAV, they actually could see even further downside in accepting new shares of FAX.

For FAX, trading at a wide discount has been quite normal, and once that merger was announced, they actually dropped even further. That is, even though the merger actually has a limited impact on FAX overall. As we noted at the open, it would add roughly $60 million in AUM for a fund that is already over $1.1 billion.

Distribution - Enticing Payout, Watching Coverage

FAX delivers a solid distribution rate to investors, coming in at 12.77%, and the NAV rate comes in at 12.05%.

That's significantly lower than FCO's distribution rate, but FCO's was incredibly unsustainable. FCO's distribution rate is also about to be either zero or in line with FAX's payout.

With that said, while it would be a relatively more moderate rate compared to its sister fund that it may acquire, it is hardly looking like it is in great shape in terms of coverage. At least, when looking at net investment income, the NII coverage came in at less than 36%.

In other words, ~64% of the payout will need to be funded via capital gains and appreciation in the underlying portfolio and investment strategy. Impacting the coverage was the result of both higher operating expenses for the fund and lower total investment income. In fiscal 2024, TII came in at $65.3 million, and that eased to $58.5 million. Total expenses rose to $29.338 million from the $24.57 million. So that really squeezed NII on both sides of the equation.

For tax purposes, they actually listed that fiscal 2025's distribution was almost entirely return of capital. This would likely be the result of significant capital loss carryforwards that were utilized. As of the end of their fiscal year, they were still sitting on around $193 million.

However, it is also the result of overpaying the distribution, after accounting for NII and the realized/unrealized gains/losses, FAX saw a decline in its NAV year-over-year.

FAX's Portfolio

As the fund's name would imply, and along with the fund's investment objective, they are heavily concentrated in Asia-Pacific fixed-income investments. In particular, the fund is heavily tilted toward holding government debt of this geographic region. The largest sector allocation is neraly 50% allocated to government debt.

Some of this debt is paying quite a high rate as well; in particular, they are holding some of Mexico's debt at an 8.5% rate. Which is actually interesting on its own because Mexico is not an Asia-Pacific nation, nor is South Africa. However, keep in mind that the policy is that they "normally invest at least 80% of their total assets". That leaves them some room to incorporate some other geographic regions within their portfolio and still be well within their investment policy.

With that being said, with some of this higher-rated debt comes relatively lower-rated credit quality within the portfolio. The fund has the highest allocation to investment-grade securities at BBB or above, coming in at 73.2%. However, BBB is only the first rung into investment-grade quality, highlighting the relatively greater risks of some of these nations despite being "government" debt.

Finally, I would highlight that given the geographic allocation of the fund, there is some currency exposure here outside of USD alone. Though USD is the largest allocation of the fund.

To help position the fund with hedges, the management team can utilize some more complex strategies than simply investing directly into underlying debt investments. That includes future contracts on U.S. Treasuries but elsewhere around the globe. Further, they also employ forward foreign currency contracts. That also helps to position them in various ways that the management team feels are appropriate at a given time.

Conclusion

FAX provides a fixed-income portfolio with a heavy concentration in Asia-Pacific countries, particularly the government offerings, as it comprises the largest allocation of the portfolio by far. On its own, FAX seems like a fairly solid fixed-income offering providing relatively unique exposure. The discount is fairly appealing here, but not particularly at a massive bargain. The distribution is quite high, which is appealing, but that will also require some significant capital gains to fully cover. So the long-term sustainability is something I would question. However, since it isn't trading at a substantial premium, that isn't a particularly significant overhanging risk if they cut their payout.

Not like we had seen with something like FCO—which FAX is set to potentially acquire this sister fund. For FAX, this would not alter the fund too materially, given the significant size difference. It also isn't necessarily guaranteed to occur, either, as investors in FCO have the choice to potentially liquidate their holdings.

At present, liquidating their entire holding at NAV would make more sense because while it is currently trading at a slight premium, FAX is at a larger discount. Essentially, investors could liquidate at NAV and then buy FAX at an even better price instead of accepting the new shares and immediately accepting those shares that are trading below NAV. With that said, we have a couple of months until the special meeting, so keeping a close eye on the discount/premium levels will be important.

data.title: Best Staples Stocks As Consumer Confidence Hits Multi-Year Low
data.description:
Consumer Staples Shift from Laggard to Leader in 2026

The consumer staples sector is off to a strong start in 2026, on pace for its best January performance in over a decade. This momentum is driven by renewed investor appetite for value and defensive stocks after years of tech’s growth dominance. After lagging broad markets in 2025 as AI-fueled gains captured headlines, staples have gained attention, as inflation moderates, rate cut expectations firm, and fiscal stimulus potentially supports consumer spending on essentials. Yet consumer confidence has dipped to its lowest level in over a decade. While I’ve recently highlighted select AI stocks that can outperform in 2026, the consumer staples sector (XLP) is significantly outperforming technology (XLK) year-to-date:

With grocery price growth still elevated and discount retail gaining share, staples can continue to grow even as consumer confidence remains challenged. Policy tailwinds like anticipated tax cuts could lift disposable income, helping sustain demand for staples in 2026, even as consumers remain cost-conscious. Investors looking for defensive exposure amid market volatility can consider opportunities not just in large cap staple giants, but in niche and value-oriented names poised to benefit from these macro trends.

How I Chose the Best Consumer Staples Stocks

While investors can easily create custom screens with the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener, there are 30 pre-screened categories of stocks, including one for the top consumer staples stocks, which I used to select the ones in this article. The stocks are automatically sorted with Quant Strong Buys at the top, and I chose four that possess Quant Factors that indicate potential for continued growth in 2026.

1. Village Farms International, Inc. (VFF)

  • Market Capitalization: $392.76M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 1 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 1 out of 11

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Agricultural Products & Services

Village Farms International operates vertically integrated greenhouse agriculture, producing fresh produce for U.S. and Canadian retailers and branching into high-growth wellness segments, including CBD products and cannabis. Its unique blend of essential products and exposure to emerging consumer health trends has helped drive 350%+ gains for VFF over the past year. Like many of the growth stocks I analyze, VFF sells at a premium P/E (non-GAAP) relative to peers, but its forward growth provides support for the higher price and potential for more room to run. Supporting VFF’s ‘A+’ Growth grade is its forward EBITDA growth metric of nearly 95%, which is more than 20 times the sector median of 4.35%

Profit margins are below sector median, but they are moving in the right direction, as evidenced by CEO Mike DeGiglio stating that "our third quarter was another one of many records for Village Farms" and emphasizing a 21% year-over-year increase in consolidated net sales and net income from continuing operations of $10.8 million, or $0.09 per share. As a small-cap in the consumer staples sector, which is skewed by mega-caps like Procter & Gamble Company (PG) and Walmart Inc. (WMT), profitability metrics typically screen below medians. A highlight for VFF is its Levered FCF Margin of 11.04%, which is more than double the sector median, signaling that the company is generating higher free cash flow after interest and debt relative to its revenue base.

Village Farms’ focus on essential food products and health-oriented goods positions it well within an inflation-sensitive macro backdrop as consumers focus on value and quality. As investors seek consumer staples with differentiated growth, VFF’s strategic mix of core agriculture and health wellness segments keeps it compelling. This creates a good starting point and transition into a smaller, direct-to-consumer supplements play.

2. Nature's Sunshine Products, Inc. (NATR)

  • Market Capitalization: $421.64M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 3 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 1 out of 31

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Personal Care Products

Nature’s Sunshine Products is a global wellness company producing and distributing a range of nutritional supplements and personal care products through a network of independent distributors. The company has delivered outsized returns relative to many sector peers, supported by growing autoship subscriptions and international diversification. Recent earnings beat expectations, reflecting resilient demand for health and immune-support products and operational improvements.

NATR’s Growth Grade is a ‘B-’ and it’s improving as it was a ‘C+’ three months ago and a ‘C’ six months ago. Reflected in the growth metrics, NATR’s 8.21% forward EBITDA growth shows tremendous improvement from its YoY measure, and is nearly 90% higher than sector peers. Looking ahead, NATR can continue to grow if recent trends continue as well. As its CFO and Treasurer Shane Jones pointed out at last quarter’s earnings call, “we continue to see improved momentum as digital accelerates, while maintaining our core business of specialty retailers, practitioners, affiliates and business builders.” Jones highlighted particular strength in North American digital business year-over-year growth, which accelerated to 52% in Q3. If that strength carries into 2026, NATR can continue its 6-month outperformance vs peers.

In the current macro environment, where consumers remain cautious but prioritize health and value, NATR’s product breadth and distributor network offer a solid platform for sustained growth. These strengths segue into beverage giants like Ambev, where brand power within staples also serves as a buffer in uncertain times.

3. Ambev S.A. (ABEV)

  • Market Capitalization: $44.85B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 2 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 1 out of 7

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Brewers

Ambev is one of Latin America’s largest beverage companies, boasting an extensive portfolio of consumer favorites across beer, such as Budweiser and Stella Artois, and non-alcoholic drinks, including Pepsi and Gatorade. Its scale, cost-efficient operations, and strong brand positioning have enabled it to navigate inflationary pressures while maintaining margins, which leads me to Ambev’s Profitability, a key strength in its Quant Factor Grades.

ABEV’s Net Income and Levered FCF margins both demonstrate its sector dominance as the metrics are each more than four times its consumer staples peers. The company’s ability to manage input costs and pass select pricing through to consumers enhances its competitive moat. In an environment where consumers trade down to value-oriented staples, Ambev’s strong regional brands and streamlined cost structure are major advantages that can keep its sector-crushing performance momentum going in 2026.

From here, this consumer value orientation sets the stage for distribution-centric staples like UNFI, which play a key role in ensuring shelf availability across food retail.

4. United Natural Foods, Inc. (UNFI)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.28B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 4 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 1 out of 7

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Food Distributors

United Natural Foods is a leading food distributor supplying grocery stores, natural food outlets, and other retailers with a broad range of staples products. As supply chains continue to normalize after pandemic and tariff disruptions, UNFI’s scale in distribution and logistics has translated into renewed operational leverage. The company benefits from steady demand for essential groceries and natural products, while increasing retailer emphasis on streamlined inventory and cost efficiency plans, to UNFI’s strengths. To illustrate, I’ll begin with UNFI’s Quant Factor Grade strength in growth.

UNFI’s forward EBITDA Growth of 12.15% is a key metric that demonstrates its sector leadership, as consumer staples peers measure at a median of 4.35%. Even more attractive, forward EPS Long Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) is expected at 79.34%, more than 11 times the sector median. UNFI’s role as a wholesale backbone gives it unique visibility into consumer purchasing trends, which drive its tailored strategies to boost margins, potentially supporting its recent performance momentum.

This profit-driven strength and defensiveness caps the individual stock section and leads naturally into a broader sector wrap about resilience and relative value appeal in the 2026 macro environment.

Conclusion: Consumer Staples Can Balance a Mixed 2026 Outlook

Collectively, these four Quant Strong Buys in the consumer staples sector illustrate the market segment’s defensive appeal and differentiated growth potential as 2026 unfolds. From Village Farms’ vertically integrated produce and wellness portfolio to Nature’s Sunshine Products’ direct-to-consumer supplements, Ambev’s beverage leadership, and United Natural Foods’ distribution backbone, each offers a unique angle on staples’ resiliency in uncertain times. While macro conditions, such as evolving consumer confidence and policy support remain mixed, staples’ relative stability and essential nature make them compelling considerations for diversified portfolios.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top 'strong buy' quant stocks. Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

data.title: 3 Reasonable Market Predictions For February
data.description:
Friday will mark the last trading day in the first month of 2026. The markets have remained quite resilient despite numerous concerns around the economy and equities. In January, Greenland and Minneapolis became two unexpected sources of headlines and turmoil.

A bit of short-lived volatility triggered by ever-changing tariff policies was a theme during January as well. The massive winter storm Fern brought snow across most of the nation and is likely to ding Q1 GDP growth by at least one half of one percent. Especially as another large winter storm is expected to hit a good chunk of the country this weekend. Despite this, we are coming to the end of the first frame of the New Year with equities trading at or near all-time highs.

So, what lies ahead for investors for the shortest month of the year? Three market predictions for February follow below.

1. The AI Story Becomes More Problematic

While equities overall are nicely up to start 2026, the stocks of some of the tech giants that have powered the rally over the past three-plus years have gotten dinged to start the new year. Oracle (ORCL) looks like it will be down more than 10% in January, and Microsoft (MSFT) is heading to a more than 10% loss for the month as well.

Investors are starting to ask pertinent questions around some of the core pillars of the AI Narrative on a more frequent basis. Questions around how the huge new electricity demands from the massive AI data center buildout will be met and whether AI-related revenues will rise enough in coming years to even begin to justify the hundreds of billions of dollars being invested annually for the AI infrastructure buildout.

You can see how these trends are playing across the ecosystem this week. Microsoft is seeing its stock trade significantly down on Thursday despite posting Q4 results that beat both top- and bottom-line expectations. Mister Softie's cloud revenue was up 29% year-over-year and came in at over $50 billion. Non-GAAP income was $38.9 billion for the quarter. However, on a GAAP basis, net income came in at $30.9 billion, $8 billion less. Microsoft's non-GAAP results exclude the impact from investments in OpenAI; it should be noted. Significant additional investment in OpenAI (OPENAI) may also be in the offing as well.

Meta Platforms (META) noted in their just-posted fourth-quarter results that it expects to nearly double capex spending due to AI in FY2026 to a range of $115 billion to $135 billion. Capex for Q4 came in at $21.4 billion, up $7 billion from the same period a year ago. It should be noted that this investment will generate little revenue, at least over the near and potentially medium term. To put in perspective, all U.S. AI revenues are not expected to broach $100 billion industry-wide until FY2027.

The company also added $6.02 billion in the quarter to the $73 billion in cumulative losses in the company's virtual reality and Metaverse efforts, aka Reality Labs. A huge investment that has not panned out to this point proves even tech visionaries are not omnipotent.

As I noted in an earlier article, Morgan Stanley this week slashed its price target on Oracle (ORCL) by one-third. Morgan Stanley noted Oracle's “rapid buildout of artificial intelligence infrastructure will likely push earnings per share results to fall below expected targets and increase capital expenditures.”

I expect scrutiny around AI capex versus AI revenues to become a much bigger topic in February and the months ahead. Will this be the prick that pops the AI Bubble? As my late father liked to say, “Only the Shadow knows.”

2. Gold Pauses

One of the big stories in January was the huge rally in gold, silver, and other precious metals. Three years ago, gold sold for just under $1,900 an ounce. It broached $5,500 an ounce early on Thursday in what has been a parabolic move this month.

Silver has had a much bigger move here in January. There have been many explanations for these huge moves. The dollar debasement/sell America theme is probably the most cited one at the moment.

Japan has seen a huge spike up in its yields on its debt, with 30-year yields hitting their highest levels ever and 10-year yields moving to their highest threshold since 1999. With a debt-to-GDP ratio of roughly twice that of the U.S. and the second oldest population of any country, this has put a renewed focus on sovereign debt across the G-20. Rising concerns around Japan was one of my three market predictions for January; it should be noted.

A piece on Seeking Alpha Thursday provided a good summary of what is driving gold prices higher from Seeking Alpha analyst Frank Holmes:

Multiple tailwinds - runaway debt, Fed credibility concerns, global central bank buying - are fueling a 'perfect storm' for gold prices.

There are certainly good reasons for rising precious metal prices. However, an approximate 60% move in silver and a better than 25% rise in gold in January have the feel of blow-off tops. I would also be concerned about the potential impacts on counterparties from those funds/investors that have been on the wrong side of these moves.

My second prediction for February is these moves in precious metals will at least pause in the coming month. I also would not be surprised to see significant profit-taking given the sharp moves up across the precious metal complex.

3. Volatility Spikes

I think spiking commodity prices are a prelude to much higher volatility in the equity markets in the month ahead. The last time precious metals had this sort of year-over-year surge was in 1979-1980. This was in a stagflationary period when the PE on the S&P 500 was in the high single digits and the misery index was a key theme within the economy and on the news.

February also could open with another partial government shutdown and/or military action in Iran. The latter being one reason oil prices are moving up sharply this week. Despite these concerns, the S&P VIX Index (VIX) has been quite calm since seeing a huge surge during the rollout of “reciprocal tariff” policies in early April. We have had a couple of spikes above 20 since then, but all of these have quickly dissipated. Including the one I predicted for January.

My last prediction for February is that this market calm will be upset at least once this month and the VIX will broach the 30 level. In addition, equity markets will see at least one day when the NASDAQ loses more than 2.4%, as it did on Jan. 20 during the peak of the Greenland worries.

And this wraps up my three market predictions for the coming month. The markets continue to trade at extreme valuation levels. Equities have remained resilient in January, and complacency continues to be a core pillar of the current investment landscape. I doubt this lasts much longer.

As Sergeant Phil Esterhaus of Hill Street Blues famously quipped, “Let’s be careful out there.”

data.title: Alger Small Cap Focus Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Update
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Alger Small Cap Focus Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Class A shares of the Alger Small Cap Focus Fund outperformed the Russell 2000 Growth Index during the fourth quarter of 2025. Exact Sciences Corporation (EXAS), Guardant Health, Inc. (GH), and Natera, Inc. (NTRA) were among the top contributors to performance.

Exact Sciences is a molecular diagnostics company focused on oncology testing, best known for its Cologuard noninvasive colorectal cancer screening franchise, alongside a broader portfolio and pipeline that includes molecular residual disease (MRD) and multi-cancer early detection (MCED) testing initiatives. Shares contributed positively during the quarter after Abbott announced a definitive all-cash agreement to acquire Exact Sciences for approximately $21 billion in equity value, reflecting a meaningful premium and driving the stock higher.

Guardant Health is a molecular diagnostics company specializing in liquid biopsy tests for oncology applications. Unlike traditional tissue-based tests, Guardant’s liquid biopsies use blood samples, enabling faster results and improved patient comfort. The company’s tests include comprehensive genomic profiling, cancer diagnosis, and treatment monitoring. Shares contributed to performance after the company delivered strong fiscal third- quarter operating results and raised full-year 2025 revenue guidance, signaling stronger-than-expected momentum across Oncology, Screening, and Biopharma & Data.

Natera is a specialty diagnostics laboratory that provides high-value genetic testing across three core franchises: reproductive health (including non-invasive prenatal testing for chromosomal conditions such as trisomy 13, 18, and 21), oncology (led by Signatera, which measures circulating tumor DNA to assess treatment response and detect molecular residual disease and cancer recurrence), and transplant (tests used to monitor organ rejection). These tests are built on the company’s proprietary cell-free DNA liquid biopsy platform, enabling highly sensitive detection from a blood sample. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, with management commentary pointing to revenue and margin performance that exceeded expectations on the back of higher test volumes and favorable reimbursement dynamics— particularly within the Signatera franchise—alongside another increase to full-year 2025 revenue and margin guidance.

Nebius Group (NBIS), uniQure N.V. (QURE), and Stevanato Group (STVN) were among the top detractors from performance.

Nebius is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated “neocloud” beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the “circularity” of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.

uniQure is a biotechnology company developing AMT-130, an investigational gene therapy for Huntington’s disease—an area with no approved disease-modifying treatments. Shares were highly volatile in 2025, surging after the company reported three-year Phase 1/2 data indicating meaningfully slower disease progression—approximately 75% versus a propensity-matched external control cohort. However, shares detracted during the quarter after U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) feedback signaled that the external-control dataset may no longer be adequate as primary evidence to support an accelerated approval filing, increasing regulatory uncertainty and likely extending the timeline despite the encouraging efficacy signal.

Stevanato is a leading supplier of pharmaceutical-grade glass packaging used to store and deliver medicines, including vials, pre-filled syringes, and cartridges across many therapies. The company also provides end-to-end solutions that support the drug life cycle, which can shorten lead times, lower total costs, and reduce supply-chain risk for customers. Shares detracted in the fourth quarter after third-quarter results benefited from shipment timing—orders that were originally expected to ship in the fourth quarter were pulled forward into the third quarter—raising concerns that fourth-quarter results would be softer. Full-year revenue guidance was unchanged, which also implied a fourth-quarter outlook slightly below investor expectations. In addition, some investors remain concerned that the growth of oral GLP-1 drugs could eventually take share from injectable GLP-1s, a recent demand driver for Stevanato’s delivery components.

data.title: Alger Small Cap Focus Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Environment

U.S. equities ended the fourth quarter on a firm footing, with the S&P 500 Index returning 2.7%, extending the steady grind upward that characterized 2025, after the tariff announcements. Investor confidence was supported by a familiar trio: corporate earnings that came in better than expected, a Federal Reserve (Fed) that pushed further into an easing stance, and a macroeconomic backdrop that — despite mounting cross- currents — remained resilient in the aggregate. Market sentiment also benefited from improving clarity on trade policy, including an extension of the U.S. – China truce and selective tariff relief aimed at easing consumer affordability pressures.

At the same time, the quarter was defined by widening dispersion beneath the index- level surface. The market’s biggest tailwind — continued investment tied to artificial intelligence (AI) — was increasingly accompanied by scrutiny around AI infrastructure bottlenecks, sources of financing, and whether the AI theme could deliver returns commensurate with the capital deployed. Macroeconomic risks also remained in play: labor market softening became harder to ignore, consumers appeared more cautious and bifurcated across income cohorts, and housing stayed under pressure.

Complicating the read-through, a prolonged government shutdown reduced economic data visibility, amplifying debate over inflation and employment signals just as global central-bank dynamics leaned less uniformly supportive. Nevertheless, U.S. equities reinforced a constructive baseline for the S&P 500 Index, where Health Care was the top performing sector, while Real Estate was the weakest.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.

data.title: SpaceX: The $1.5 Trillion Lifeboat For Tesla's Failing EV Business
data.description:
Introduction

I had been waiting for Tesla, Inc.’s (TSLA) earnings for the last few weeks, and I hoped to hear any new information about the Starlink (STRLK) IPO. Instead, CEO Elon Musk did not even mention his space initiatives and focused solely on Tesla's results, which are, to put mildly, controversial. Even though he did not straightforwardly connect the two companies, I believe the ground for the future $1.5 trillion IPO is being set up already today.

In this article, I want to take a look at some of the metrics from Tesla’s earnings that point to a possible partnership of Tesla with the newly public company, whether it will be Starlink or, what is more likely, SpaceX (SPACE).

Let’s Take A Look At The Q4

My colleague has done an amazing job breaking down Tesla’s earnings right after it went out, so I won’t be repeating all of the headline numbers, but I’ll share a few of them that made me really interested in where it’s all headed right now.

In my opinion, the most historic and important part of the earnings call was the fact that Tesla finally admits its EV part of the business isn’t going as well as planned, and takes down two of its flagship models - Model S and Model X.

It's time to basically bring the Model S and X programs to an end with an honorable discharge because we're really moving into a future that is based on autonomy. And so if you're interested in buying a Model S and X, now would be the time to order it because we expect to wind down S and X production next quarter and basically stop production of Model S and X next quarter. We'll obviously continue to support the Model S and X programs for as long as people have the vehicles. But we're going to take the Model S and X production space in our Fremont factory and convert that into an Optimus factory, which will -- with the long-term goal of having 1 million units a year of Optimus robots in the current S and X space in Fremont. - Elon Musk.

The company, of course, connects it to the fact that they could use a production line for the Optimus robot, but Tesla has never really been a cost-conscious company when it comes to Elon Musk’s ideas. Historically, whenever Musk comes up with a new vision, the company is willing to commit significant capital and resources to pursue it. So even this apparent shift in thinking, or in how the company approaches doing business, doesn’t look like something that would realistically happen. For example, back in 2018, the production of Model 3 had been paused a couple of times because of Musk's vision of futuristic automation, and the excessive amount of capital that was spent to achieve the goal, which turned out to be unachievable later on.

Today’s guidance for the CapEx spending on the new vision of Elon Musk supports my previous claim. The CEO is very excited about the future, leaving the EVs behind, focusing on Robotaxis, and hopes to take this company into an entirely new dimension with automation and AI.

We're making very, very big investments. So this is going to be a very big CapEx year as Vaibhav will get into. That is deliberate because we're making big investments for an epic future.

So we're also going to be significant manufacturers of solar cells, and we're making massive investments in AI chips.

In general, even as Musk did not mention the new IPO or the plans, I think the ground for the partnership between Tesla and Musk’s space projects is being prepared already today. This could not only help Tesla smooth the transition away from the EV narrative and keep the trust of the believers, but also attract those investors into the new IPO. The company is actively looking forward to creating its own chips, and Musk already thinks about launching data centers in space. The discussion of this topic has been going on for some time now.

The solar opportunity is underestimated. We think the best way to add significant capability to the grid is -- or not only to the grid, let's say, powering AI data centers is solar and batteries on earth and solar in space. So that's why we're going to work towards getting 100 gigawatts a year of solar cell production integrating across the entire supply chain from raw materials all the way to already finished solar panels.

In my opinion, Tesla deserves to be a $130 billion company for all of its futuristic ideas and plans, but its current market cap of $1.4 billion laughs at my conclusion, as Elon continues to virtuously promise the moon (quite literally). I don’t believe in the robtaxi moat, as Waymo is way ahead technology-wise, and also in launching those, and frankly, I would not invest in Alphabet (GOOG) solely for Waymo. I don’t think this technology merits Tesla’s valuation, and I think the adoption will be slow. At the same time, I recognize the marketing moat, and I think Tesla could stay at these expensive valuation levels if the $1.5 trillion IPO of SpaceX is made this year.

Let’s Talk About The SpaceX IPO

It’s still very early to take a look at the actual financials, as there has been no filing of the S-1 with the Securities and Exchange Commission or the SEC. This document gathers the crucial information of the business that plans on going public, including its business model, goals, financials (which would be especially great to see), risks, etc. At the same time, the IPO of this size and visibility can’t be done quietly, so there is enough public information to speculate on the timing, and even what numbers we would possibly see there.

The first thing that may be confusing for investors, and most likely for the management of SpaceX, is whether to spin off Starlink as a separate IPO or to take it public under the SpaceX umbrella. The dilemma is valid, as one of them is the business that already shows impressive growth and numbers, and this part will look especially great for potential investors. The other part has a highly risky business model, that’s capital intensive, and the results there won’t be easy to predict quarter-over-quarter. Keeping Starlink together with SpaceX will help SpaceX to show the predictable, recurring revenue from satellites (projected to reach $15–$24 billion in 2026), and by this, to partly justify an enormous market cap of $1.5 billion.

According to Reuters, last month in the secondary selling of the shares, SpaceX got to the valuation of $800 billion, which makes it one of the largest deals in history. But this is the valuation for a private SpaceX. When the company goes public, it usually get’s 20%–30% premium only for being more liquid, so that already pushes it past $1 trillion. All of the rest will come from the investor’s excitement, from pricing in the future expectations, etc. Musk previously wanted to keep SpaceX private, but space-related stocks had recently been in the headlines, rallying to extreme valuations, as the government started being interested in different projects as well.

Additionally, now will be a good time to take the company public, as Starlink doesn’t yet have any real competition, and it is showing very rapid growth in the number of satellites in orbit. The investors tend to reward this metric, although in my previous article about Amazon’s (AMZN) space project, Leo, I explained why it’s actually a disadvantage.

Amazon, by the way, is also rapidly expanding its presence in space, and I believe it will begin competing with Starlink for market share as early as next year. While Starlink holds a significant first-mover advantage and has delivered tangible results, Amazon can leverage its AWS and Prime ecosystems as a launchpad for a much more rapid adoption. I expect Amazon’s growth to outpace Starlink’s trajectory early in the days, both because of these built-in advantages and the fact that consumer demand for satellite internet is already more established.

Looking at the numbers Starlink posted in their 2025 progress presentation, it’s clear the company has turned into a global utility from just an ambitious, futuristic startup. In 2025, it crossed 9 million customers, nearly doubling its base. It says it has more than 9 thousand active satellites, which cover 155+ countries. They are embedding themselves into sectors that are hard to reach, like aviation, maritime, and agriculture, which is great because it makes them a part of the global transportation and emergency services. Most of the competitors will try to pursue these markets.

The acceleration in the hardware is particularly impressive. The V2 Mini design added over 270 Tbps of capacity this year, and with V3 satellites targeting over 1 Tbps per satellite in 2026, the network is scaling rapidly.

Interestingly, Starlink is now the primary source of SpaceX's financial health. It is projected to generate roughly 70% of the company's total $15.5 billion revenue for 2025. It has 10 million subscribers on the horizon for 2026, and Starlink already achieving standalone profitability since 2024, so we’re looking at a company with serious recurring revenue.

Besides, throughout this presentation, you will find dozens of mentions of the “Direct to Cell.” This is a development that helps to connect a regular phone to the satellite with no additional gear, and this industry has many disputes among investors, and what it will eventually do to the regular phone connectivity providers. Right now, it’s already been used by 12 million people to stay connected in "dead zones," and as it upgrades to full 5G speeds, it could potentially lead to a multi-billion dollar revenue stream. Unlike launching rockets, which is expensive and risky, this is a software-driven service where users pay a monthly fee, so this is a high-margin, recurring income that investors reward generously, and this will be the main driver of this IPO’s valuation.

Why SpaceX is Tesla’s Lifeline

It’s clear Tesla is not a winning business today, in 2026. The main segment is shrinking, the margins are deteriorating not only in cars, but also in services, and some of the areas that are growing are being very far from offsetting the losses. The management succeeds, as it usually happens, in taking the attention of the investors from the problems to the promises of the bright future. The problem is that even if Robotaxis launches, based on the results of Waymo, the management has to understand it won’t fix Tesla’s problems. So it means they need something else to keep people listening. This something else is energy, AI, and… as of right now, SpaceX IPO.

Rumors are that the IPO will have priority access for current shareholders of Tesla. This automatically creates a massive marketing moat for Tesla’s stock price. Even if the car business is shrinking, people will hold TSLA just to get to the SpaceX IPO. The rumors are supported by Elon Musk's post on X from 2024:

Another argument is AI. Tesla said on earnings that it wants to spend over $20 billion on CapEx this year, much of it for AI compute. In the 2026 Davos forum, Musk said that power is the bottleneck for AI, and by launching "Orbital Data Centers" (solar-powered AI chips in space), SpaceX provides the infrastructure that Tesla needs to run its autonomous cars. Here we can already see how the vertical integration narrative shapes up, and Wall Street usually loves to see it.

Finally, according to the Financial Times (reported by Reuters), the IPO is most likely to happen in the middle of 2026, probably in June. A few specific dates are being discussed the most; the first is closer to the beginning of the month for a rare conjunction of Jupiter and Venus (June 8-9), and the other is June 28, for Elon Musk’s birthday. It’s very possible the S-1 could be filled soon, and the fact that no new information was presented at the earnings could mean the start of the Quiet Period.

Conclusion

The SpaceX IPO is already one of the most discussed financial events of 2026, despite the fact that an S-1 has not been filed yet. The SpaceX story really looks compelling right now, and not only because it is a company of Elon Musk, unlike Tesla. SpaceX has successfully transitioned from a high-risk startup into a global utility with an enormous infrastructure moat.

However, I can’t help but look at the parallels with Tesla's history. Tesla performed brilliantly until the moment of the start of the competition. Today, Starlink is the first-mover with zero serious rivals, but that window is closing. Giants like Amazon, with its massive AWS and Prime ecosystems, are preparing to enter the market with the ability to scale and connect billions of users much faster than Starlink did in its early days. Furthermore, the "future" parts of the SpaceX story, like the orbital data centers and space-based AI, remain highly uncertain. There is no guarantee SpaceX and Tesla will hold a moat in these areas against established tech leaders.

Ultimately, this IPO arrives at the perfect time for Elon Musk. With Tesla’s core EV business shrinking and its valuation resting almost entirely on future promises, the SpaceX listing could serve as a critical lifeline to Tesla. It allows Musk to keep the Tesla "believers." The execution of this IPO will likely be a success in terms of keeping Tesla’s valuation expensive and the narrative alive, but once the competition starts, I would be approaching SpaceX more carefully. As you’ve probably realized by now, I don’t see any reason to invest in Tesla.

data.title: 2 Top 2026 REIT Picks: One For Safety, One For Aggressive Investors
data.description:
As we go into 2026, many of you are asking me, "What are the best REITs to buy for 2026?"

It's a tough question to answer, as it depends entirely on what you're looking for.

There's not one REIT (VNQ) that checks all the boxes and suits every type of investor.

There are some REITs that are great for more conservative investors seeking safe income, while others are better suited for more aggressive investors attempting to maximize total returns.

In today's article, I'm going to give you two of my top picks for 2026. The first one will be the safer option, while the second one will be the riskier but potentially even more rewarding choice.

Both are very attractive, depending on your risk profile, and we own both of them in our portfolio at High Yield Landlord.

Combined together, they currently offer an 8% average dividend yield at the moment, and we expect significant upside in 2026.

Kimco Realty (KIM): Safer Pick

I recently explained that strip centers are one of my favorite REIT sectors in 2026 to invest in.

The reason why is that these REITs are today out of favor and trade at very reasonable valuations, and that's despite these properties being undersupplied, enjoying steady rent growth, and having high occupancy rates.

In comparison, apartment REITs, for instance, are today trading at higher valuations, even despite facing oversupply, stagnating rents, and declining occupancy rates.

Best of all, the new supply of such retail properties is likely to remain low for many years to come because new development projects are not economically feasible based on today's rents.

This means that the rents of these properties are likely to keep growing steadily for years to come.

But what's the best strip center REIT to buy today?

I think that it's Kimco Realty (KIM). It's one of the "blue chips" of this property sector with its A-rated balance sheet, large scale, and focus on more defensive grocery-anchored and service-oriented properties.

This has historically led it to trade at a premium valuation relative to its peers like Whitestone REIT (WSR) and Kite Realty Group (KRG) during most times.

But recently, its stock has underperformed, and this has led its valuation to drop toward the bottom of its peer group:

REIT Name FFO Multiple
Regency Centers (REG) 14.9x
Federal Realty Trust (FRT) 13.5x
Whitestone REIT (WSR) 13x
Kite Realty Group (KRG) 11.3x
Kimco Realty (KIM) 11.3x

This is truly exceptional for Kimco and would seem to imply that its recent results must have been poor.

But in reality, it's the opposite. The REIT has not yet released Q4 results, but earlier this year, it hiked its full-year guidance, now expecting to grow its FFO per share by 6% this year, which is one of the fastest growth rates in its property sector. Moreover, it also hiked its dividend by another 4%, all while also initiating a new share buyback plan. They probably could have hiked the dividend even more, but given how cheap the stock has become, they prefer to focus on buybacks to unlock value.

And 2025 is unlikely to be an outlier.

The REIT's current average rent is about $20 per square foot, but they're signing new leases in the $25-30 range at the moment.

This means that the REIT is likely to enjoy steady growth in the years ahead as its leases gradually expire and are resigned at higher rates.

So why is it suddenly so cheap?

I think that it's because the REIT has more debt than usual maturing in 2026, and this will likely slow down its FFO per share growth this year. This is enough of a reason for the market to discount the REIT, as most investors are heavily focused on short-term results:

I would note, however, that this is nothing catastrophic.

We're talking about just 12% of its debt maturing, and this is a REIT with an A-rated balance sheet and a very reasonable 5.3x debt-to-EBITDA.

Outside 2026, its debt maturities are well staggered, meaning that it should enjoy steady growth beyond that, especially so if interest rates keep dropping to lower levels.

Therefore, I see this as a rare opportunity to buy an A-rated blue-chip operating in an undersupplied property sector at a discounted valuation.

Trading at an estimated 30% discount to its net asset value, I believe that Kimco could enjoy up to 40% upside from here as the market moves past its near-term concerns and its growth accelerates.

While you wait, you earn a 5.1% dividend yield that's safely covered and growing. That makes the risk-to-reward very compelling for more conservative investors, in my opinion.

NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP): Riskier Pick

Now we move to the riskier pick, and with that comes a much greater yield and higher upside potential.

NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP) is currently offering an 11.3% dividend yield and up to 75% upside potential.

Naturally, you don't get such high expected potential returns without taking significant risks.

The risk here is that the REIT focuses on cannabis cultivation facilities, which have historically been the riskiest property sector of all, with cap rates as high as 15%, and regular tenant bankruptcies and lease defaults.

The main issue in this property sector is that most cannabis operators are on shaky legs. They're barely profitable, if at all, and often rely on capital raises to make rent payments.

However, as we discussed in a recent article, the conditions could materially improve already in the near term.

President Donald Trump just recently signed an executive order to reschedule cannabis as a less dangerous, Schedule III drug.

This is huge for cannabis operators because it should significantly increase their profitability.

As a Schedule I drug, cannabis companies were heavily punished by the IRS as they were not able to deduct regular business expenses such as payroll and rent, resulting in effective tax rates as high as 80%.

This means that the profitability of these businesses should surge if and when cannabis is finally rescheduled. Adjusted for that, the rent coverage ratios of this sector are actually even higher than those of other net lease REITs like Realty Income (O):

The 4.2x rent coverage ratio shown in the slide above is today only theoretical, as most profits are going towards taxes, and it's making it very difficult for these companies to sustain operations.

But as this changes, the risks of this sector will decrease significantly. Suddenly, even the weaker tenants will have a lot more breathing room, and with profitability surging, I also expect a lot more VC capital to make its way into this space, allowing these companies to raise more equity and debt to solidify their balance sheets and position them for a brighter future.

If this happens, I would expect REITs like NLCP to surge in value because the only reason why they are priced at such a low valuation and high yield is the high risk of the tenants. The REIT has faced some lease defaults in recent years, and it has caused its cash flow to stagnate. Remove this risk, and the REIT will likely manage to return to growth and attract a lot more capital from investors, given how cheap it is.

Simply repricing at 12x FFO, it would need to rise by 75% from here, and it would still trade at a discount to most of its net lease peers.

Closing Note

At High Yield Landlord, we hold Kimco in our Retirement Portfolio, which aims to maximize safe long-term income. It does not offer the highest yield, but it is growing steadily and has a defensive business model.

NewLake, on the other hand, we hold in our Core Portfolio, which aims to maximize total returns, at the cost of higher risk, of course. Some of these riskier picks will not work out over the long run, but that's why we diversify accordingly, currently holding 23 companies in our portfolio.

data.title: L'Oréal: A Resilient Beauty Giant At A Fair Price
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

L'Oréal (LRLCF) (LRLCY) has been performing well recently, maintaining a very strong financial position that could help them withstand and even take advantage of the currently weak consumer environment (as they did with a big deal recently), although the valuation already seems to take it into account, with little margin of safety while the environment could get worse.

L'Oréal's Q3 was solid overall, with double-digit growth in online and a like-for-like 4.2% increase in sales, with strong performance especially in their Professional Products - outperforming the market, with great momentum seen especially in Europe and emerging markets, driven by significant e-commerce and selective distribution acceleration and salon market reignition focus - and Dermatological Beauty.

The company's second half tends to be better in terms of free cash flow, with €2.74 billion reported in H1'25 compared to €1.99 billion in H1'24, but €6.64 billion in 2024 as a whole, with solid momentum in H1 despite the tariff uncertainty persisting.

Financially, based on L'Oréal’s latest financial report, we continue to see a very strong position, with the current assets covering not only their current liabilities but also a good chunk of their few non-current ones, with limited debt given their size (a big advantage compared to peers), offering a massive advantage and significant financial flexibility, especially since they had €4.82 billion in cash and cash equivalents.

Regarding the dividend, L'Oréal pays a 1.78% yield now, meaning about $4.59 billion in 2025, as well as $1.18 billion spent on buybacks over the past 12 months, for a modest combined yield of ~2.36%.

L'Oréal is expected to release their Q4 report on February 12, and I expect the company to report relatively unimpressive numbers, as the environment is likely going to keep pressuring them in the near term, similar to how LVMH (LVMHF) also mentions that this weakness will continue in 2026, which I covered recently in this article. Also, as this is the fourth quarter, it has the potential to be more volatile given the potential to see the company issue guidance for 2026.

Risks & Opportunities

As they highlight, the US beauty market is now normalizing following the disruption seen during the pandemic and the boom after it, although that doesn’t mean it will be a straight line from here, and there’s certainly the possibility of more pressure. The company’s Dermatological Beauty performance is a good example of this, with the CEO commenting the following during their Q3 2025 Sales/Trading Call:

But the market in Dermo has been slowing. It's globally at plus 5% at the end of after 9 months. And it's mostly the North American market that has slowed down with weakness in drug stores and probably a bit cautious consumption on this category of product.

The consumer environment has been under pressure following the pandemic and subsequent rate hikes, with nearly half of Americans reportedly facing more stress heading into 2026 compared to the beginning of 2025, with fears around inflation looming while discretionary budgets were affected at the same time as the cost of living increased.

Although L'Oréal is able to tap into international markets for better rates, there’s also the risk of seeing the long-term US treasuries (which impact SOFR) stay higher for longer even if the Fed continues cutting rates, which can come as a result of the market adjusting the yields it demands to take into account all the recent new risks.

These include the renewed tariff fears (although Greenland-related ones cooled down, we now have 100% tariff threats on Canada), as well as worries about the Fed’s independence once Powell’s term ends in May 2026, plus the weakening dollar that’s almost reaching 4-year lows recently and rising national debt - so it’s no wonder that gold just passed $5,600 per ounce at the moment of writing this.

It’s true that a devaluing dollar could be good if the country wants to repay its national debt, but at the same time, the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget (CRFB) recently warned about the risks stemming from the potential to see this debt growing faster than the US economy, warning that this could trigger “six distinct types of fiscal crises.”

Similar to LVMH, this capital migration can be a mixed factor for L'Oréal, as even though it can hurt their US operations, this capital migration could help foreign assets, with the two companies being giant, dependable, high-quality European players that can easily attract more interest from funds. Again, two Nordic pension funds (Sweden's Alecta and Denmark's AkademikerPension) have recently sold or were in the process of selling their US treasuries, which can be only the beginning of a wider trend that can affect US equities.

Especially as the US is the top beauty market, the current macro pressure can have even more weight, and the company has talked about this extensively, including during their most recent Global Beauty Discussion event at the beginning of December, where the CEO made the following comment:

I mean there's -- the terminology we often hear is this question of recovery, but I would just maybe remind that the first quarter in the U.S. was the new administration when they took office at that time. And there was a lot of, I'd say, confusion in the market and particularly amongst consumers, especially around immigration policy around tariffs, around inflation and -- so it was not a typical quarter. That's for sure. And the acceleration from that quarter has been, I think, rather extraordinary.

Still, the company has a very solid portfolio, with brands across most price segments that can help them withstand economic downturns while being tailored for a wide variety of generations/age groups, all while growing their e-commerce presence and dominating platforms like Amazon's (AMZN), Ulta Beauty's (ULTA), Target's (TGT), or even Walmart's (WMT), with the CFO saying that they “want to be where the consumers want to buy.”

In the future, L'Oréal is likely going to keep using their strong momentum to keep acquiring and developing brands, similar to what the other giants in the industry do, as they are able to integrate these brands into their global logistic chains and use their know-how and budgets to help them expand significantly, delivering synergies and fueling the company’s growth.

Similarly, L'Oréal recently announced that they’re going to double their stake in Galderma Group AG (GALDY) to 20% at an undisclosed value, increasing their exposure to the fast-growing skincare drug market, especially through injectable aesthetic treatments used in medical spas and dermatologist offices.

This comes a few months after the $4.7 billion deal with Kering (owners of Creed fragrance, Gucci, Bottega Veneta, and Balenciaga), acquiring Kering's Creed brand and a 50-year agreement for exclusive rights, securing licenses for perfumes under the Balenciaga and Bottega Veneta brands, as well as the crown jewel, the license for Gucci that is currently held by Coty but expires in 2028 - expected to close in H1 2026 and helping Kering fix their balance sheet. Again, this is why their financial position is an advantage in this market, as the company has plenty of room for deals like these.

Risks Reiterated

Despite an overall strong portfolio addressing a wide range of consumers, the company is not immune to consumer weakness, and there’s certainly potential to see it continue in the near future, which can affect the company’s growth prospects.

Meanwhile, the macro environment can also advance towards a scenario that puts more pressure on the consumer and the company, which can affect the market’s expectations and valuations.

Valuation

For L'Oréal's DCF to equity valuation, given the company’s strong performance over H1 2025 and solid growth reported in Q3, as well as the seasonally higher numbers in H2, slightly offset by tariffs, I’ll start with an FCF of €7.5 billion in 2025 (vs. €6.64 billion in 2024), meaning about $8.97 billion when converted into USD. Given their strong position and momentum, I’m going to assume that they can deliver a CAGR of 8% (not far from the market’s expectations even for the revenue and the company’s 5-year performance), as the environment has potential to improve especially in the US and China on top of their recent acquisitions driving synergies, followed by a solid 5% as their momentum continues and a 3% terminal growth rate, with a discount rate of 9% discount rate for a decent margin of safety, using 532.96 million shares outstanding as of their Q2 report (the most recent data).

This way, we get to an equity value of $203.95 billion, for an intrinsic value of $382.67 per share compared to $448.08 for LRLCF (~$76.53 vs. $91.59 for LRLCY) at the moment of writing this, which doesn’t seem that attractive now, although we’ve been even below that point less than a year ago.

Conclusions

I rate L'Oréal a Hold, as the company is indeed strong and in an excellent financial position given their size, but the valuation already seems to take that into account and be around what I’d consider a fair level, while wider macro pressures can cut these expectations short.

They are certainly strong, and there’s plenty of room for them to take advantage of this environment, but the overall risk-reward doesn’t seem that attractive anymore, but it’s definitely worth watching for now.
